questions.question: What is 'frequency' in a dataset?
questions.correct_answer: The number of times a particular observation occurs
questions.explanation: Tally marks are used to count the frequency of each data point.

questions.question: How do you calculate the Arithmetic Mean?
questions.correct_answer: Sum of all observations divided by the total number of observations
questions.explanation: It provides the average value of the data set.

questions.question: What is the 'Range' of data?
questions.correct_answer: The difference between the highest and lowest observations

questions.question: What is the 'Mode'?
questions.correct_answer: The observation that occurs most frequently
questions.explanation: A dataset can have more than one mode if multiple values have the same highest frequency.

questions.question: How do you find the Median?
questions.correct_answer: Arrange data in order and find the middle value
questions.explanation: The median divides the data into two equal numerical halves.

questions.question: What does a 'Scale' represent in a bar graph?
questions.correct_answer: The value assigned to a unit length of a bar
questions.explanation: For example, 1 unit length could represent 5 students.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Double Bar Graph?
questions.correct_answer: To compare two sets of data for the same categories
questions.explanation: It allows for easy visual comparison of changes or differences.

questions.question: What is the probability of an event?
questions.correct_answer: Number of favorable outcomes divided by total possible outcomes
questions.explanation: Probability is always a value between 0 and 1.

questions.question: When is an event considered 'Certain'?
questions.correct_answer: When its probability is equal to 1
questions.explanation: An impossible event has a probability of 0.

questions.question: Which measure of central tendency is best for 'most popular' items?
questions.explanation: Mode shows what happens most often, like the most popular shirt size.

notes.title: Tally Mark Rule
notes.content: Every 5th mark is a diagonal line across the previous four vertical lines.

notes.title: Representative Values
notes.content: Mean, Median, and Mode are all different ways to describe the 'center' of data.

notes.title: Median Preparation
notes.content: Never calculate the median without first sorting the data in ascending or descending order.

notes.title: Equally Likely Outcomes
notes.content: When tossing a fair coin, getting a Head or a Tail are equally likely outcomes.

notes.title: Choosing a Scale
notes.content: Pick a scale that makes the tallest bar fit on the graph but still clearly shows small differences.

facts.fact: The Mean always lies between the greatest and the smallest observations of the data.
A dataset with two modes is called 'Bimodal'.
Bar graphs must have bars of equal width with equal spacing between them.
Probability helps in predicting trends in weather, sports, and insurance.
